item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who the f███ would think that food from artificial plants made from anomalous clockwork was a good idea? Since I doubt that SCP-2189-F could be distributed through grocery stores (at least they should have standards that prohibits selling some anomalous s███ made from clockwork, however in the world with no cannon they could theoretically sell them), they were probably distributed through street vendors (surely it's Safe, uncontrollably sold, with SCP-2189-F jamming and killing people. Very Safe). (If I understand right, damaging one piece of a fruit will remove the anomalous properties from all its pieces and therefore can remotely kill people who ate parts of it?) I that the article forgot to mention purchasers who broke SCP-2189-F they bought before eating. Or purchasers who bit an instance and then broke it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the original source, which can no longer be accessed, shows no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is interesting and clearly has a lot of effort behind it, but in its current incarnation I just can't give it an upvote.

- I heavily dislike the end on an O5 "firing" a site director over essentially not having figured out an SCP fast enough. This is ludicrous to me, scientific inquiry takes time (and failure) to produce better results, even from the best minds at the foundation. The pettiness I feel from that addendum flies in the face of the professionalism of the Foundation, especially at that high of a rank within it.

- The three types of fruit chosen are confusing to me, and a bit jarring? As they each grow in very different types of climate and conditions (when non-anomalous, that is). I just don't understand why there was a need for all of them to even be there. (Also rip no clockwork oranges, as has been mentioned, heh)

- The multiple, long, outdated containment procedures could be spoilered in links to give it better flow, perhaps? I know I hate having to scroll forever to get to the "real" containment procedures, and feel like my brain has to readjust each time I digest a containment description, then get to the next one and only then realize the one I just read was out of date. I'd far rather know from the get-go, as a reader, that I'm reading a since-updated procedure that may no longer apply—and I'm pretty sure Foundation personnel would prefer that the actual, current containment procedure be quickly and easily ascertainable.

- Many of the sections of this are really long for the payoff you get out of them. Literally just not telling the reader as MUCH as you're doing would make it more interesting, to me—cut it down, let the weirdness show without overexplaining it, make the reader have to try to fill in the gaps in their own head.

- One of the things I like here is the note to the nephew from the eccentric engineer—it tells a lot in its brevity, and it makes the subject feel real. More shorter sections like this, to tell the whole thing in more interesting, easier-to-digest (pun intended) pieces, maybe?

I can't upvote this as it is—but i like the concept/backstory enough to really want to see a rewrite here!

item.title: Definitely Underrated
item.content:encoded: Wow. My initial read left me more curious and even confused than impressed. However, after reading the author's explanation in laymen's terms (which I greatly appreciate as a simpleton :b) posted previously in this discussion, I have to say that this is quite amazing. It certainly deserves more attention, and way more upvotes.

item.title: Reclassification
item.content:encoded: Why did this get reclassified as Keter? What makes it that dangerous and hard-to-contain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a little bit difficult to figure out what this thing is all about (I thought it was CotBG at first), but after you get it the whole article is pretty good. +1

item.title: An apple a day keeps the doctor away!
item.content:encoded: And of course by doctor I mean site director. I'd say this is a tale meshed into an SCP done right; subtle, but interesting and stand-alone without the tale. Easy +1, kind of surprising it's only at 10.

item.title: Well
item.content:encoded: I guess you could say they underestimated an apple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was worried about making things too obvious on the one hand, and too obscure on the other. It sounds like some people got it, some didn't. If reading the spoilers below gives you any ideas for how to make it clearer without spoon-feeding it, I'm happy to hear them:

+ Core concept
- The zone will be one of danger

This engineer-cum-occultist switched the innards of a group of timepieces and a group of trees, without changing their outer nature (much). This was a practice run for his plan to switching his own "inner workings" (including his brain) with those of an ancient Lovecraftian being made out of clockwork, that he discovered sleeping underground. In theory, this would turn him into "the Sleeping King," while the Sleeping King would be stuck in his hapless, mortal human form.

The O5 did a quick upgrade to Keter on the assumption that the guy's plan was to wake up after becoming the Sleeping King (and because of the possibility that drilling into it might help that to happen). Since the Sleeping King is now full of human-stuff, the guy's plan was at least partly successful. Whether he will wake, and what happened to the guy's body, remains unknown because of the Foundation's own missteps, for which the Site Director got sacked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still think this needs work, because I honestly have no idea what exactly is going on here. I don't know why it's Keter, I'm fuzzy on what it does, and multiple rereads haven't helped.

I really, really want to like this. I can see a lot of potential in both the article and the author. But it still needs a lot of work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That major update did it. This thing is marvelous. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And now with that update this has become one of my favorite SCP's. Kind of hope this guy evades the Church of the Broken God's wrath1 long enough to produce some more interesting results.

Footnotes
1. For the heresy of conflating machine and flesh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great ideas and there are some good draws, but it is still a bit too wall of text for me at this point. +1 to encourage more tightening up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Second effort. :-) And yeah, I mostly cleaned it up with my own ideas this time.

I thought of giving a heading to the final text box, but I really don't want to telegraph the "Site Director getting sacked" punchline anymore than I already have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I forget, is this a first effort? If so, it's one hell of a first SCP after the update. I don't know if you used the chatroom at all, but I would advise you do so in the future. I also think the last note needs a little more context (for example, head it with "MEMORANDUM FROM O5-X TO DR. BLACKBOX1 REGARDING SCP-2189). However, you've cleaned this up on your own rather nicely. Good job.

Footnotes
1. I'm on mobile, so actual, proper blackboxes aren't happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon entering R████'s home, the Agents discovered over three dozen clocks and watches. All of those that retained legible manufacturer markings were later identified as being of purely mechanical design, without electrical components. Approximately two dozen instances were found with their outer components (casings, faces, etc.) in a state of extreme corrosion inconsistent with their apparent date of manufacture and conditions of storage. These instances lacked internal components, and were covered in mold.

It's a little vague if "instances" refers to the fruit, or to the watches mentioned earlier in the paragraph. I know it refers to the watches, but there is still some ambiguity.

Other than that, good job on the rewrite. I like how this has become a story where the foundation screwed up.

EDIT: wording

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, a major update has been posted. Hope it helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome idea, if you can make that work. Looking forward to reading it already :D

item.title: Not Related To The SCP
item.content:encoded:
—I've logged the formatting error with WikiDot. It appears to be related to the INCLUDE statements on newly edited pages.

Whee!—

Fixed. If you spot it elsewhere the page probably needs "touched" to be fixed.

http://feedback.wikidot.com/bug:956

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback, everyone!

The bit about the clockwork popping up in someone who fully digested the fruit was an attempt on my part to give the SCP some more human impact. At the same time, I knew that it would increase the similarities with 217, and so I didn't want to go too far down that road. (I knew in advance that some folks would consider this scip to be stepping on 217's toes no matter what I did. But Roget's clockwork apple image just stuck in my head and I knew it needed to have an SCP written around it.)

I'm working on a revision currently to address the multiple comments about the story needing something more. It should be posted shortly. I notice that the article's at -7 currently. In case it hits -10 before I post the revision, mods please consider this my formal request for stay of deletion per the Deletion Guide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here actually. In fact I found it pretty interesting, but that's just me. I upvoted this because the clinical wording is good, and the idea was intriguing for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That picture just grosses me out. I don't know why.

As for the article itself, I second Taggy the most. It's the object itself that needs something… more, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually rather like this one. Its simple, clever, and just weird enough to be interesting. I especially like the bit about it being functionally identical to fruit as long as the mechanisms don't jam; brings to mind some very interesting mental images and theories on what's going on with these things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this SCP has potential. I would like to know more about its origins and long term effects. A little more backstory would make this a lot more compelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If not as a scip, this would probably work quite well as a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what? This would actually work excellently as an origin for SCP-217, maybe with the forbidden fruit angle someone mentioned upthread. I'm assuming this will get deleted, because it's currently at -7. If it does, I'd like to collaborate with the original author on a rewrite or at least encourage the author to try again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would've up voted hard if it were Clockwork Oranges! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From this page:

Microscopic brass gears were discovered in some samples of D-51259’s adipose and lymphatic tissue, and her liver was found to contain a cluster of five interlocked gears ranging in diameter from 0.1-0.4mm, the largest of which was attached to a 0.3mm-long camshaft.

From 217:

SCP-217 has even been shown to totally convert the inside of the body before showing any externally-visible symptoms.

I wasn't saying it was a direct connection, I was just noting the similarity.

EDIT (hit post instead of preview): I read it that these gears grew inside the D-Class, kind of like the clockwork organs that 217 grows in hosts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except they don't transmit anything. If these were 217 vectors, their eaters, pickers and holders would become clockwork. That ain't happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's SCP-217. Downvoted for that reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought we already had something likes this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the article, and don't see anything overly wrong with it. I agree that it could use a little bit more to it, and it is no way the best article on the site, but I found it worthy of a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why no clockwork oranges?

Back to the article though, it's fruit that transmits the clockwork virus. While this could work, it would probably work better as a tale relating to 217.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the statement that this SCP needs something more. It is clearly well thought and well developed, and you obviously put a lot of effort on it, so I'm sure you can get something to work here.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. Neutral vote for these reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As Tag said, the tone is fine and you obviously put a lot of work into it. However, you didn't really give it much of an interesting effect. While I liked the imagery of the clockwork fruit, and how you described the effect of the fruit being eaten/jammed and such, I don't think that's enough to carry an article. What you have here, though, is a good start. Maybe you could tie it into the broken god, with some kind of forbidden fruit angle, or maybe have the clockwork apples build something greater. There are a lot of directions you could take this. I originally downvoted, but since I really don't have anything I dislike about it, other than the length being too long for what it actually ends up being, neutral voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the tone is decent, and I can see you put effort in it, the object is just not really interesting. However, I think you can write something good. Try to get critique on the chat and forum next time. I expect something great from you next time.
